blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Palimpsest (noun)  - a manuscript page that has been written on, scraped off, and written over. The original text is never fully erased. Traces remain visible beneath the new.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Memory works the same way. Every time you remember something, you write over it. The version you hold now is not the original  - it's the latest draft, layered on top of all the others. But the earlier versions bleed through. You can never be sure which layer you're reading.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: This is a game about writing a eulogy. About reaching for memories and finding them changed. There are no right answers  - only the ones you choose to keep.

choices.label: Begin.
Begin (skip this next time).

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The desk lamp hums. Outside the window, the city is quiet in the way it only gets at 2 AM  - not silent, just holding its breath.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The funeral is in seven hours. You have a blank page and the wreckage of a relationship you've never been able to summarize in a single sentence.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: They were the most important person in your life. They were also the one who hurt you most. These are not contradictions. That's the problem.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You need words by morning. Something to stand up and say to a room full of people who knew a version of them. Maybe not your version. Maybe not the real one.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: You're not sure there was a real one. That's why this is so hard.

choices.label: Close your eyes. Reach for a memory.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Two memories surface at once. They always compete  - the warmth and the wound, rising together like two drafts of the same sentence.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You can only reach for one first. The other will come later, colored by whatever this one leaves behind.

choices.label: The morning they taught you something. Sunlight on the table. Their patience.
The night everything went quiet. The room where you understood.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: A Saturday. Early. They were already up  - coffee made, something spread out on the kitchen table. They looked up when you came in and said: sit down, I want to show you something.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You don't remember what it was anymore. Something practical  - how to fix a thing, or read a thing, or make sense of something that had seemed impenetrable. What you remember is their hands moving slowly, the patience in it, the way they assumed you could learn.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: For an hour, the world was small and warm and legible.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: There's a part you leave out. Midway through, a phone rang. Their face changed for half a second  - something tight and private  - before they silenced it and turned back to you. You never asked who called.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You've polished this memory until it glows. It's the one you bring out when someone asks what they were like. But even here, that half-second flicker. The face beneath the face.

choices.label: You write about that morning. The warmth. The patience. The version people need.
You write about the phone call too. The crack in it. The part you usually skip.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Not an argument. You could have survived an argument.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You were in the same room. You said something  - asked something ordinary  - and they answered with a flatness that told you everything the words didn't. Like the conversation was a duty they were performing.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You tried again. A different angle. Same flatness. The room filled with something invisible and heavy.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The night didn't end with a door slamming. It ended with you both pretending to sleep. The silence between you was the loudest thing they ever said.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: You've told this story as though it came out of nowhere. But you know that's not true. There were weeks before it. Months, maybe. You just didn't look directly at them.

choices.label: You write about that night. How it felt to watch someone leave without moving.
You write around it. The months before. What led there. Not the wound itself.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You type a few sentences. Delete one. Type it again with different words that mean the same thing.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The eulogy is supposed to be about who they were. But who they were keeps shifting depending on which memory you're standing inside.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Another memory pushes in. You let it.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: The phone call. You've been avoiding this one.

choices.label: The phone call.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: There was a phone call. Late afternoon, or maybe evening  - the light was going. One of you called the other after weeks of nothing.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You remember the sound of their breathing before they spoke. The way the silence on the line felt different from the silence in person  - thinner, easier to mistake for connection.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Something was said about wanting to fix things. Or about being too tired to keep trying. The words don't stay still. Every time you reach for them, they rearrange.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Memory doesn't record phone calls. It records how they made you feel. Then it builds the conversation around the feeling.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: What you remember most clearly is hanging up and sitting very still, holding something heavy that you couldn't name.

choices.label: You called them. You were reaching out. You're sure of that.
They called you. Even near the end, they were the one who tried.
You don't remember who called. You've told it both ways.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The page has a few paragraphs now. You read them back and they sound like someone else wrote them. Someone more certain than you.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The clock on the wall ticks. Outside, a car passes with its windows down, trailing music that disappears around a corner.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You keep writing. Another memory rises  - the promise.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: This is the one that still has weight. The one you carry whether you want to or not.

choices.label: The promise.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: They made you a promise once. Not casually  - they looked at you and said it like they were placing something fragile into your hands.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You can't say the exact words anymore. But you remember the weight of them. The way the air in the room changed. You believed them completely, because not believing would have meant seeing something you weren't ready to see.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The promise was broken. Or it wasn't. Depending on how you define the terms.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Promises are just sentences. What gives them weight is what happens after.

choices.label: They tried to keep it. Life just made it impossible.
They meant it when they said it. They just didn't mean it enough.
You made it impossible for them to keep. You set conditions no one could meet.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You stop typing. Read what you have.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: It's coherent. It's even moving, in places. But it reads like a draft of someone else's eulogy  - the safe version, the one that won't make anyone in the pews uncomfortable.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You could stop here. Clean it up, practice it once, deliver something respectable by morning.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Or you could keep going. Into the parts that are harder to hold. The parts that might be the only true thing you've ever said about them.

choices.label: Keep it clean. The funeral isn't the place for complications.
Go deeper. If not now, when?
Write the version you actually want to say. Even if you never read it aloud.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The last time you saw them.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You didn't know it was the last time. Nobody ever does, and that's what makes it unbearable  - the ordinary way it ended. A doorway. A coat being put on. Something said over a shoulder that you can't reconstruct no matter how hard you try.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You've replayed it hundreds of times, looking for the moment you should have known. The sign you should have read. But the memory gives you nothing. It was just a day. Just a goodbye that sounded like all the others.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: You keep editing the ending. Adding weight to words that probably had none. Making the last look linger longer than it did.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The truth is, you weren't paying attention. You were thinking about something else.

choices.label: Something kind was said. You're almost sure of it.

addMemory.label: The last goodbye. Something kind, or so you tell yourself.

choices.label: It was tense. You both knew something was wrong and neither said it.

addMemory.label: The last goodbye. Tension neither of you named.

choices.label: Nothing. You can't remember. That's the worst part.

addMemory.label: The last goodbye. You can't remember what was said.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The thing you never said to them.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: It lived in your throat for years. You rehearsed it in the shower, in the car, in the space between deciding to call and putting the phone down. You knew exactly how it started. You never got past the first sentence.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Now they're gone and the words have nowhere to land.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: A eulogy is just the last draft of everything you never said while they could still hear it.

choices.label: I forgive you. I don't know if you needed to hear it, but I needed to say it.
I needed you to see what you did. Not to punish you. To know that I saw it.
I'm sorry. For the version of me that made this harder than it had to be.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The window is turning grey. Dawn, or the suggestion of it.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The eulogy is on the screen in front of you. Pages, or fragments, or just a scattered constellation of moments that don't add up to a person. They add up to the space where a person used to be.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: In a few hours you'll stand in front of people who loved them  - who loved a version of them  - and try to say something true.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: What you have isn't true. But it's honest, in the way that trying is honest. You reached for them in the dark and this is what your hands came back with.

choices.label: Read it back one more time.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You read what you've written. All of it. The layers are visible  - the early drafts bleeding through the latest version, the scratched-out words still legible beneath the new ones.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: A palimpsest. That's what this is. Every memory you reached for, you changed by reaching. Every sentence you wrote replaced the one that was there before. The original is gone. Maybe it was always gone.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: The question was never what really happened. The question was always which version you could live with.

choices.label: It's almost morning.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The light is coming through the window now. You save the file.

choices.label: Continue.
Continue.
Continue.
Continue.
Continue.
Continue.
Continue.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You stand at the podium. The room is full  - people who knew them, people who loved them, people who loved a version of them that may or may not have existed.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You read what you wrote. The real version. The one with the cracks left in.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You say: they were not easy to love. Loving them was like holding something beautiful and sharp  - you did it carefully, knowing you'd bleed. You did it anyway, because the alternative was letting go, and letting go would have meant admitting that the sharpness was all there was.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You say: it wasn't all there was. They were kind in ways that surprised you. Patient in ways that cost them. Broken in ways they couldn't show you, and you were broken in ways you couldn't show them, and between the two of you there was something that looked nothing like the word love but was the closest either of you got.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Someone in the back row shifts uncomfortably. Someone in the front row is crying.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: It's not the whole truth. There is no whole truth. But it's the closest you've come to saying something honest about them  - and the closest you've come to saying it about yourself.

choices.label: Begin again with a different first memory.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You stand at the podium. The room is waiting.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You read the version that everyone needs to hear. The one full of warmth and light and the specific details that make people nod and smile through tears.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You talk about the Saturday mornings. The patience. The way they could make a room feel safe just by being in it. You leave out the phone calls that changed their face. The silences that lasted weeks. The promise.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: It's beautiful. People come up to you afterward and say: that was exactly right. That was exactly who they were.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You nod. You accept the hugs. You drive home alone.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Maybe mercy is its own kind of honesty. You gave them the version that lets everyone grieve cleanly  - the version that doesn't ask anyone to hold two things at once. Maybe that's enough. Maybe it has to be.

choices.label: Begin again with a different first memory.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You stand at the podium. Somewhere between the second and third paragraph, the eulogy becomes something else.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: It becomes about you. What you failed to say. What you withheld. The ways you made the relationship harder than it needed to be because you were afraid of something you couldn't name.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You say: I wasn't easy either. You say: I kept score. I punished silences with longer silences. I held standards I couldn't meet myself and called it integrity.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The room is very quiet. This is not the eulogy they expected.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: But the dead don't need eulogies. The dead don't need anything. This was never for them.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: Maybe what you needed to write was not who they were but who you were when you were with them. The version of yourself you've been avoiding since the night you found out they were gone.

choices.label: Begin again with a different first memory.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You stand at the podium. The page is in your hand.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You open your mouth. Nothing comes.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The page has words on it  - you wrote them, you know you did  - but they look like someone else's handwriting now. The sentences don't connect. The memories don't add up. Every version you tried to commit to dissolves when you look at it directly.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The room waits. Ten seconds. Twenty. Someone coughs.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: What comes out is not the eulogy. What comes out is: I don't know how to talk about them. I've been trying all night and I don't know how. Every time I reach for who they were, I get a different person. I can't hold them still long enough to describe them.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You fold the page. You sit down.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: It was the most honest thing you said all night. Not because it was true  - you don't know if it was true  - but because it was the only thing you said that you didn't write over first.

choices.label: Begin again with a different first memory.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You write it. All of it.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: The broken promises. The silences that were punishments dressed as patience. The way they could make you feel invisible while standing right next to you. The specific, surgical cruelties that only someone who knows you can perform  - because only someone who knows you knows exactly where to cut.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You write until the window is bright and your hands are shaking and the page is so full of truth it's almost unbearable to look at.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: Then you select all. Delete.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: You write something polite. Something warm. Something with their name in it that sounds like a person everyone could love. You save it. You print it.

blocks.speaker: narrator
blocks.text: At the funeral, you read the polite version. It goes over well.

blocks.speaker: brain
blocks.text: But the real version existed, once, on a screen at 4 AM, and for a few minutes it was the truest thing you'd ever written about anyone. The fact that you deleted it doesn't mean it wasn't true. The fact that it was true doesn't mean you owed it to anyone. Writing it was the point. The fire was the point.

choices.label: Begin again with a different first memory.
